reasoning: The time horizon is one week (from Sunday, March 29, 2026, to Sunday, April 5, 2026). Given that Easter 2026 falls on April 5, Good Friday will be observed on April 3, 2026, meaning the US stock exchanges will be closed that Friday. Therefore, the resolution price will be determined by just four trading days of trading (March 30 through April 2). Over such a short time horizon, stock price movements are largely consistent with a random walk plus noise, clustering tightly around 50%. The linear statistical model estimates a 53% probability of the price increasing, driven by strong recent upward momentum (the stock rose from $77.33 on March 20 to $81.99 on March 27). The automated statistical summary’s combined estimate of 0.43 is downwardly biased by an invalid seasonal comparison (evaluating 2025's absolute stock price against 2026's levels, rather than comparing week-on-week percentage movement). Barring major scheduled catalysts—and noting no unpriced dividends or earnings fall in this exact window—there is no extremely strong signal to deviate significantly from even odds. Balancing the historical short-term drift and recent momentum with the intrinsic market randomness of a 4-day trading week, I estimate the probability of the stock closing higher to be 51%.
